pull.title: doc: clarify test placement guidance
pull.body:
### Problem
The developer notes do not currently explain how test coverage should be placed across a commit stack, or how that relates to the simpler single-commit case where a new test can be run against the old implementation.
This leaves room for [review friction](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35251#discussion_r3217842286) around current-behavior tests, regression tests, and behavior-preserving refactors.
This was also discussed a few years ago in https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/31212#discussion_r1854105033

### Fix
Add a short `General Testing` section with a `Commit Structure for Tests` subsection.
It clarifies that behavior-changing commits should carry the relevant coverage, that behavior-preserving refactors can omit tests when there is no externally observable behavior to test, and that characterization tests can make stacked changes easier to review by recording uncovered current behavior before a later behavior change.

events.body: The following sections might be updated with supplementary metadata relevant to reviewers and maintainers. ### Code Coverage & Benchmarks For details see: https://corecheck.dev/bitcoin/bitcoin/pulls/35260. ### Reviews See [the guideline](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#code-review) for information on the review process. | Type | Reviewers | | ---- | --------- | | ACK | [optout21](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35260#pullrequestreview-4262942676), [rkrux](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35260#pullrequestreview-4263672831) | | Concept ACK | [hodlinator](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35260#pullrequestreview-4263695330) | If your review is incorrectly listed, please copy-paste <!--meta-tag:bot-skip--> into the comment that the bot should ignore.
Seems fine, but for single commit prs, testing can also be done by not compiling the cpp code changes (or reverting them) and then running the test as-is and observing a failure.

ACK 3df19b4e0aa90206197fcebbb27950133f46b91c

Prev:
reACK b2cb955d518e03321e06fd0516c48a2b8e762b6b
ACK 737e5a488aa0e9ad1245e087c87aa3c465327c15
I consider the proposed approach good software craftsmanship. The text is well explained, and the 'why' question is supported by references.

There is a relevant quote in the mentioned blog post that I feel worthwhile to reproduce here:

> When a system goes into production, in a way, it becomes its own specification. We need to know when we are changing existing behavior regardless of whether we think it’s right or not.

> testing can also be done by not compiling the cpp code changes

I agree, especially for a small single-commit fix where reverting the code change is straightforward.

The proposed split is meant to make the evidence more granular. With a combined fix+test commit, reviewers can check that the bundle fails without the fix and passes with it, but the test itself does not stand alone as a record of prior behavior. With a separate current-behavior test commit, that commit documents what the code did before, and the following fix commit shows the minimal expectation change.

I agree that this matters most once the fix is larger than a one-line change. For refactors or multi-file changes, there often is no clean "just revert the cpp" recipe, and mixing the fix with several new assertions can make it harder to see which assertion changed and which ones were already true.

That is the characterization-testing angle: the existing system "becomes its own specification", so the split helps show exactly when behavior is being changed.

Adjusted the PR description, covered this angle, added @optout21's suggestions, thanks.

verification.payload:
tree b65eaecfbbbaeb662f2c548b801008e491ebbaab
parent 1af8e0c4e895ebd159304ed86ac059bebeced1f8
author Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1778494826 +0200
committer Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1778499570 +0200

doc: clarify test placement guidance

Add a short developer-notes section describing where test coverage usually belongs in a commit stack.

Clarify that a single behavior-changing commit can often be checked by running the new test against the old implementation, while a separate current-behavior test commit can make stacked changes easier to review by recording the prior behavior before the expectation changes.

Replace the existing CONTRIBUTING.md sentence with a link to the new developer-notes section, so the detailed guidance lives in one place.

Co-authored-by: MarcoFalke <*~=`'#}+{/-|&$^_@721217.xyz>
Co-authored-by: optout <13562139+optout21@users.noreply.github.com>

Add a short developer-notes section describing where test coverage usually belongs in a commit stack.

Clarify that a single behavior-changing commit can often be checked by running the new test against the old implementation, while a separate current-behavior test commit can make stacked changes easier to review by recording the prior behavior before the expectation changes.

Replace the existing CONTRIBUTING.md sentence with a link to the new developer-notes section, so the detailed guidance lives in one place.

Co-authored-by: MarcoFalke <*~=`'#}+{/-|&$^_@721217.xyz>

events.body:
lgtm ACK 3df19b4

I haven't gone through the links in detail but I understand the general idea.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -298,6 +298,25 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## Tests and Commit Structure
comments.body: I have doubts that allocating a full top-level ("##") section to this issue is the best. Maybe the section could be called "General Testing", with the first paragraph, and the second paragraph demoted by one level to "### Commit Structure for Tests".

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -298,6 +298,25 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## Tests and Commit Structure
+
+Tests should be added or updated for new features, bug fixes, and other behavior
+changes. A behavior-changing commit should include the relevant test coverage.
+Test changes can be omitted for behavior-preserving work with no externally
+observable behavior to test, such as moving code, renaming, or mechanical
+refactors.
+
comments.body: The general points on testing could also include that tests should check results or post-conditions (a test without proper checks increases code coverage, but has limited value). But this is already outside of the scope of the original intent.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -298,6 +298,25 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## Tests and Commit Structure

@@ -298,6 +298,25 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## Tests and Commit Structure
+
+Tests should be added or updated for new features, bug fixes, and other behavior
+changes. A behavior-changing commit should include the relevant test coverage.
+Test changes can be omitted for behavior-preserving work with no externally
+observable behavior to test, such as moving code, renaming, or mechanical
+refactors.
+

@@ -298,6 +298,30 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## General Testing
+
+Tests should be added or updated for new features, bug fixes, and other behavior
+changes. A behavior-changing commit should include the relevant test coverage.
+Test changes can be omitted for behavior-preserving work with no externally
+observable behavior to test, such as moving code, renaming, or mechanical
+refactors.
+
+### Commit Structure for Tests
+
+A good rule of thumb is to put tests in the commit whose behavior they prove. In
+a single behavior-changing commit, the relationship can often be checked by
+running the new test against the old implementation and observing it fail. In a
+stacked change, if the current behavior is not already covered and a later
+commit intentionally changes it, consider first adding a behavior-preserving test
+commit that documents the current behavior, even if that expectation will change.
+This [characterization testing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Characterization_test)
+style keeps the evidence more granular: the test-only commit records prior
+behavior, and the behavior-changing commit shows the minimal expectation change;
+see also [Michael Feathers' original
+write-up](https://michaelfeathers.silvrback.com/characterization-testing).
+When prior behavior is already covered, update those tests in the same commit as
+the behavior change.
comments.body:
Reminds me of:

```
CONTRIBUTING.md:125:This means tests must be updated in the same commit that changes the behavior.
```

Maybe that line can be replaced by a link to this section?

Not sure what should go in `CONTRIBUTING.md` vs this file here.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -298,6 +298,30 @@ Linux: `sudo apt install doxygen graphviz`
 
 MacOS: `brew install doxygen graphviz`
 
+## General Testing

+Tests should be added or updated for new features, bug fixes, and other behavior
comments.body: Also, adding a test is not always the best choice. Sometimes it is better to manually document how to test something, instead of adding a brittle and confusing test for eternity.
